title: My body knows how to rest
subtitle: A short affirmation for trusting the body's natural capacity for sleep and restoration.
excerpt: A short affirmation for the anxiety that sleep will never return. The body has been sleeping and resting since birth; sleep difficulty doesn't erase that knowledge, it buries it. This affirmation reinstates the fact. Use in the morning, or before sleep, or any time the fear that you'll never sleep properly again arrives.
sourceNotes: Adapted from Day 1 of SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive (Rebecca J Page, 2025), "Start where you are" reframe. The affirmation shape (present-tense, positive construction, repetition) follows public-domain affirmation craft, with roots in the New Thought movement of the late 19th century.

content.text: A short affirmation for the fear that sleep will never return. The body has been resting and sleeping since birth; ongoing sleep difficulty doesn't erase that capacity. It obscures it. The sleeplessness is a temporary state, not a permanent condition. This affirmation reinstates what the body already knows.

Sleep difficulty creates a specific fear: that the body has forgotten how to sleep, that the pattern is broken permanently, that last night is what every night will be. This fear compounds the difficulty, the worry about not sleeping keeps the nervous system alert, which makes sleep harder, which confirms the fear. The affirmation interrupts that loop by reinstating the fact.

attrs.quote: My body knows how to rest.

content.text: Three times, slow. Hand on the centre of the chest if the words need a physical anchor. Say it out loud in the morning, in your mind at bedtime. Both work. Let the word "knows" land. Not "will remember", not "is trying to". Knows. Present tense, present fact.

Use this affirmation every day for one week regardless of how the previous night went. The morning after a good night, the morning after a hard one. Consistency matters more than the quality of the night before.
If the fear is too loud for the affirmation to land, tap first.
Tapping to start where you are
meets the exhaustion and fear directly before the reframe. For the night-time version of the same resistance, when the effort of trying to sleep is keeping you awake,
I release the need to force sleep

Adapted from Day 1 of SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive by Rebecca J Page. The reframe "my body knows how to sleep" closes the daytime tapping script there. The affirmation shape, present-tense and positive, follows public-domain affirmation craft.
